title: Room-temperature shape-memory effect in Sr(Ni$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$P$_2$
authors: Juan Schmidt, Alexander J. Horvarth, Seok-Woo Lee, Sergey L. Bud'ko, Paul C. Canfield
abstract: The compound SrNi$_2$P$_2$ can exhibit multiple crystal structures with no P-P pairs bonded (uncollapsed tetragonal, or ucT, state), with one-third of the P-P pairs bonded (one-third collapsed orthorhombic, or tcO, state), or with all P-P pairs bonded (collapsed tetragonal, or cT, state) across the Sr layers. The system can be tuned into its different states by changing temperature, mechanical stress, or chemical composition. Changes in bonding may manifest in changes of macroscopic properties of the material, such as its shape, electrical conductivity, or magnetism. In this work, we show that SrNi$_2$P$_2$ can be tuned among the three states by changing Cu substitution and temperature. We present temperature-dependent resistance and single-crystal x-ray diffraction results in Sr(Ni$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$P$_2$ single-crystals that show that Cu substitution favors the P-P bonding, stabilizing the cT state at ambient pressure. We construct a $T-x$ phase diagram that shows how all of these transition temperatures increase with increasing Cu fraction, $x$. The transition between the tcO state and the cT state exhibits a very large thermal hysteresis, which can be tuned to temperatures close to room temperature. In particular, the properties of Sr(Ni$_{0.963}$Cu$_{0.037}$)$_2$P$_2$ may make it suitable for applications as a shape memory material at room temperature.
summary: This paper explores the tuning of structural phase transitions in Sr(Ni1-xCux)2P2 through chemical substitution. By increasing the Cu fraction, the researchers were able to stabilize the collapsed tetragonal phase and shift the thermal hysteresis of the structural transition to encompass room temperature. The study demonstrates a significant shape-memory effect with high recoverable strain and low fatigue. This makes the material a promising candidate for practical engineering applications involving smart materials.
main_problem: Investigating how Cu substitution in SrNi2P2 can tune structural phase transitions and stabilize the collapsed tetragonal (cT) state to achieve a room-temperature shape-memory effect.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that Cu substitution favors P-P bonding, allowing the tcO-cT transition hysteresis to span room temperature, making Sr(Ni0.963Cu0.037)2P2 a candidate for room-temperature shape-memory applications.
method: Single-crystal growth via Sn flux, followed by structural characterization using X-ray diffraction, transport and magnetic measurements (resistance and magnetization), and nanomechanical compression tests on micro-pillars.
model_or_system: The physical system is the intermetallic compound Sr(Ni1-xCux)2P2, which features three structural phases (ucT, tcO, and cT) characterized by varying degrees of P-P bonding across Sr layers.
key_observables: Lattice parameters (a, b, c), P-P bond distances, transition temperatures (T1, T2c, T2w), thermal hysteresis width, and recoverable strain.
important_parameters: Cu substitution fraction (x), temperature (T), and applied mechanical stress.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the relationship between nominal and measured Cu fraction; Figure 6 presents a T-x phase diagram highlighting the hysteresis and room-temperature target region; Figure 10 compares phase stability between Rh and Cu substitution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the structural phases of SrNi2P2, details the chemical substitution and crystal growth methods, presents the temperature-dependent structural and transport results, constructs a T-x phase diagram, and concludes with mechanical testing demonstrating the shape-memory effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Main panel: temperature-dependent resistance normalized by its value at 300 K measured upon cooling for Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2; a constant offset of 0.1 was added between each curve in order to visualize them more clearly. Inset: magnetic susceptibility measured under a ZFC protocol with a field of 20 Oe applied perpendicular to c for the x =0.069, in order to estimate a superconducting volume fraction of less than 0.04. The temperature history protocol in which the datasets were taken is discussed in the main text.

FIG. 1. Fraction of Cu in Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2 determined by EDS, xEDS, as a function of the nominal Cu fraction that was originally present in the high-temperature solution that the crystals were grown from. The red line represents the linear fit, for which a zero intercept was forced.

FIG. 4. (a) Temperature-dependent magnetization normalized by the field of 10 kOe applied perpendicular to the crystallographic c-axis, measured upon cooling and warming for Sr(Ni0.989Cu0.011)2P2; the ranges corresponding to the transitions T2w and T2c are shaded in gray.

FIG. 3. (a) Temperature-dependent magnetization normalized by the field of 10 kOe applied perpendicular to the crystallographic c-axis, measured upon cooling for Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2; a constant offset of 0.00005 emu/molT M was added between each curve in order to visualize them more clearly. (b)-(d) Photos (with increasing magnification) of the shattered crystal fragments for x = 0.016 after cycling through the cT transition.

FIG. 5. (a-h) Temperature-dependent lattice parameters and (i-p) P-P distances of Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2 with (a,e,i,m) x = 0, (b,f,j,n) x = 0.016, (c,g,k,o) x = 0.037, and (d,h,l,p) x = 0.069, determined by single-crystal x-ray diffraction; d(1) P-P and d(2) P-P correspond to the shorter and longer distance between P atoms across the Sr layers, indicated with magenta arrows in the structure diagrams of panels (l) and (p), respectively. The values of b are not displayed given that they are equal to a for the ucT phase, and to 3a for the tcO phase.

FIG. 6. Temperature-composition phase diagram of Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2. The transitions determined from resistance, XRD and magnetization measurements are represented with open circles, solid triangles and solid circles, respectively. The triangles pointing down and up were obtained upon cooling and heating, respectively. The white, green and blue regions correspond to the ucT, tcO and cT phases, respectively. The gray region delimited by the two dashed lines (determined by T2w and T2c) corresponds to the hysteresis region, where either tcO or cT can be metastable. The red dotted line indicates the room temperature, and the compositions highlighted with the red oval and solid red line correspond...

FIG. 8. Uniaxial stress versus strain curves measured along the c axis on single-crystalline micropillars of Sr(Ni0.963Cu0.037)2P2 at room temperature. (a) Results obtained on the as-grown sample before heating, on the first compression (magenta solid line), first release (magenta dashed line), second compression (green solid line), and second release (green dashed line). (b) Similar results obtained after heating the sample to 373 K and then returning to room temperature.

FIG. 7. Dependence of the (a) a and (c) c lattice parameters, as well as (b) the P-P distances across the Sr planes, as a function of the Cu fraction, x in Sr(Ni1−xCux)2P2. (d) Schematic diagram of the tcO structure indicating a, c, d(1) P-P and d(2) P-P. (e) Schematic protocol to illustrate the shape-memory of the sample with x = 0.037 when combining changes of pressure with changes of temperature.
FIG. 9. Combined T −x phase diagram for Sr(Ni1−xMx)2P2, with (a) M = Co increasing to the left, and (b) M = Cu increasing to the right.
FIG. 10. Combined T −x phase diagram for Sr(Ni1−xMx)2P2, with (a) M = Rh increasing to the left, and (b) M = Cu increasing to the right.
